core_thesis: The report argues that legacy VPNs are fundamentally inadequate against AI-powered attacks because they create blind spots, slow patch cycles, and encourage risky user workarounds, making them the fastest path to breach. It advocates for a zero-trust architecture (specifically Zscaler Private Access) to provide least-privileged, user-to-app access with full visibility and containment.
detailed_summary: The Zscaler ThreatLabz 2026 VPN Risk Report, produced in partnership with Cybersecurity Insiders, highlights the growing danger of relying on traditional VPNs in an era of AI-driven cyberattacks. Key statistics reveal that 61% of organizations have faced AI-enabled attacks in the past year, and 79% believe attackers exploit vulnerabilities faster than patches can be deployed. The report emphasizes that VPNs act as blind encrypted pipes—70% of respondents have limited or no visibility into AI threats traversing their VPN, and 1 in 3 inspect none of the encrypted traffic. This lack of visibility, combined with slow patching (54% need a week or more to patch a critical vulnerability) and user bypass of VPN controls (63% do so for speed), creates conditions where attackers can move laterally undetected. The report concludes that zero-trust solutions like Zscaler Private Access (ZPA) are necessary to reduce exposure and limit blast radius by enforcing user-to-app, least-privileged access.

entities.name: Zscaler ThreatLabz
entities.role: Security research division of Zscaler that produced the report

entities.name: Cybersecurity Insiders
entities.role: Partner in producing the VPN Risk Report

entities.name: Zscaler Private Access (ZPA)
entities.role: Zero-trust solution recommended as an alternative to VPNs

key_data_points: 61% of organizations reported AI-enabled attacks in the last 12 months (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: indicates that AI is now a common attack vector, so you should incorporate AI-driven techniques like automated phishing or vulnerability scanning into your red team exercises).

54% of organizations need a week or more to patch a critical VPN vulnerability (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this slow patch cycle creates a window of opportunity for exploiting known CVEs; prioritize VPN vulnerabilities in your attack simulations).

77% of organizations say they cannot contain lateral movement after a VPN compromise (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: once you breach a VPN, lateral movement is likely to succeed; plan post-exploitation paths accordingly).

79% of survey respondents believe AI lets attackers exploit vulnerabilities faster than patches can be deployed (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: emphasizes the need for speed in your attacks; use automated tools to accelerate exploitation).

70% of respondents admit limited or no visibility into AI threats moving over their VPN (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this blind spot means your malicious traffic over VPN is less likely to be detected).
1 in 3 organizations inspect 0% of encrypted VPN traffic (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: encrypted tunnels are a perfect cover for exfiltration or C2; use strong encryption to evade inspection).

63% of users bypass VPN controls to reach apps faster, creating unmanaged risk (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: shadow IT and bypasses create additional attack surfaces; target these unmanaged access points).
conclusions: Legacy VPNs are no longer sufficient against AI-speed attacks due to inherent blind spots and slow response times. The report's data shows that attackers exploit the gap between vulnerability disclosure and patching, and VPNs provide no visibility into encrypted traffic, allowing lateral movement. For red teamers, this means VPNs are a high-value target with a high probability of success.

Zero-trust architectures like ZPA mitigate these risks by enforcing least-privileged access and inspecting all traffic. The report strongly advocates for replacing VPNs with such solutions. As a red teamer, understanding zero-trust weaknesses (e.g., misconfigurations, identity-based attacks) will be crucial for future engagements.
personal_relevance_summary: This report is highly relevant to your red teaming and cybersecurity interests because it quantifies the weaknesses of VPNs—a common enterprise perimeter technology. The statistics provide concrete attack vectors: slow patching, lack of encrypted traffic inspection, and user bypasses. You can use these findings to justify focusing on VPN exploitation in your red team exercises, such as targeting unpatched CVEs or leveraging encrypted tunnels for C2. Additionally, the report's emphasis on zero-trust alternatives gives you insight into the evolving defense landscape, helping you anticipate and test modern security controls.
extra_advice: Consider building a red team lab that simulates a legacy VPN environment (e.g., OpenVPN, Pulse Secure) and practice exploiting known vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2024-21887, CVE-2023-46805). Then, test your ability to move laterally undetected through encrypted tunnels. Also, experiment with AI-driven tools like automated vulnerability scanners or phishing frameworks to replicate the speed advantage attackers have.
